## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

content.text:
That's rsync's `--itemize-changes` (`-i`) output. Each string is an 11-character flag field of the form `YXcstpoguax`.

## The 11 positions

Position 1 — **update type** (what's happening):
- `<` — file transferred **to** the remote (sending)
- `>` — file transferred **from** the remote (receiving)
- `c` — local **change/creation** (dir created, symlink, device made)
- `h` — hard link
- `.` — no transfer, only attributes changed
- `*` — a message follows (e.g. `*deleting`)

Position 2 — **file type**:
- `f` — regular file
- `d` — directory
- `L` — symlink
- `D` — device
- `S` — special file (socket, fifo)

Positions 3–11 — **what differs** (per attribute):
- `c` — checksum/content differs (regular files), or change for other types
- `s` — size differs
- `t` — modification time differs (`T` = time set to transfer-time instead)
- `p` — permissions differ
- `o` — owner differs
- `g` — group differs
- `u` — access/modify/create time (reporting)
- `a` — ACL differs
- `x` — extended attributes differ

Each of these positions shows either the letter (that attribute changed), `.` (unchanged), or `+`.

## Your two examples

`cd+++++++++`
- `c` — created locally
- `d` — it's a directory
- `+++++++++` — **newly created**, so every attribute is "new" (nothing to compare against)

→ **A directory that didn't exist on the target and was just created.**

`<f+++++++++`
- `<` — file sent to the remote
- `f` — regular file
- `+++++++++` — newly created on the receiver, all attributes new

→ **A brand-new file transferred to the target.**

The `+` in every attribute slot is rsync's shorthand for "this item is being created fresh," as opposed to something like `>f.st......` which would mean an existing file being updated because its size and time differ.

5	## User Scenarios & Testing

8	
9	Lineage: 001-US-4
10	
11	The system implements a six-level logging hierarchy (DEBUG, FULL, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL) with independent level configuration for file logging, terminal UI display, and external library filtering. Log levels follow the ordering DEBUG > FULL > INFO > WARNING > ERROR > CRITICAL: DEBUG is the most verbose and includes all messages, while FULL is a high-verbosity operational level that does NOT include DEBUG-level diagnostics. Logs are written to timestamped files in `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/` on the source machine. 12	
13	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by:
14	1. Running sync with various log level configurations

21	
22	1. **Given** user configures `log.file: FULL` and `log.tui: INFO`, **When** sync runs and a job logs at DEBUG level, **Then** the message does NOT appear in the log file nor in the terminal UI (DEBUG is excluded by FULL)
23	
24	2. **Given** user configures `log.file: INFO`, **When** sync runs and a job logs at FULL level (e.g., "Copying /home/user/file.txt"), **Then** the message does not appear in either log file or terminal UI
25	
26	3. *(Removed)*

The hostname mapping (source hostname and target hostname) is logged once at session start, not in every entry.

36	
37	Lineage: 004-US-1
38	
39	As a pc-switcher user, I want to specify log levels in my configuration file so that I can control the verbosity of file output, TUI output, and external library noise independently.
40	
41	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by creating a config file with specific log levels and verifying that log output respects those levels.

74	2. **Given** a module is configured with a specific log level, **When** that module logs a message below its threshold, **Then** the message is filtered out.

90

92	
93	- What happens when the config file contains an invalid log level string?
94	  - System MUST fail with a configuration error (consistent with other config validation).
95	  - Lineage: 004-edge-cases
96	
97	- What happens when a third-party library uses print() instead of logging?
98	  - Ignored. Well-maintained libraries use proper logging. No stdout/stderr capture (YAGNI for an interactive CLI tool).
99	  - Lineage: 004-edge-cases
100	
101	- What happens when log levels are omitted from config?
102	  - System should use sensible defaults (file: DEBUG, tui: INFO, external: WARNING).
103	  - Lineage: 004-edge-cases
104	
105	- What happens when log volume is very high (e.g., FULL level during large sync)?
106	  - Performance should not degrade significantly; the logging pipeline should remain async.
107	  - Lineage: 004-edge-cases
108	
109	## Requirements
110	
111	### Functional Requirements
112	
113	#### Log Level Hierarchy
114	
115	- **LOG-FR-HIERARCHY**: System MUST implement six log levels with the following ordering and semantics: DEBUG > FULL > INFO > WARNING > ERROR > CRITICAL, where DEBUG is the most verbose. DEBUG includes all messages (FULL, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL, plus internal diagnostics). FULL includes all messages from INFO and below plus operational details, but excludes DEBUG-level internal diagnostics.  
116	  Lineage: 001-FR-018 -> 004-FR-006
117	
118	**Log Level Definitions** (from most to least verbose):
119	- **DEBUG**: Most verbose level for internal diagnostics, including command outputs, detailed timings, internal state transitions, and all messages from lower levels (FULL, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL). Intended for deep troubleshooting and development.
120	- **FULL**: High-verbosity operational details including file-level operations (e.g., "Copying /home/user/document.txt", "Created snapshot pre-@home-20251115T143022") and all messages from lower levels (INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL). Excludes DEBUG-level internal diagnostics.
121	- **INFO**: High-level operation reporting for normal user visibility (e.g., "Starting job X", "Job X completed successfully", "Connection established") and all messages from lower levels (WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL).
122	- **WARNING**: Unexpected conditions that should be reviewed but don't indicate failure (e.g., config value using deprecated format, unusually large transfer size) and all messages from lower levels (ERROR, CRITICAL).
123	- **ERROR**: Recoverable errors that may impact sync quality but don't require abort (e.g., individual file copy failed, optional feature unavailable) and CRITICAL messages.
124	- **CRITICAL**: Unrecoverable errors requiring immediate sync abort (e.g., snapshot creation failed, target unreachable mid-sync, data corruption detected). Triggered when jobs raise an unhandled exception.
125	
126	#### Level Configuration
127	
128	- **LOG-FR-FILE-LEVEL**: System MUST allow configuring the log level floor for file output (`log.file`).  
129	  Lineage: 001-FR-020 -> 004-FR-001
130	
131	- **LOG-FR-TUI-LEVEL**: System MUST allow configuring the log level floor for TUI output (`log.tui`).  
132	  Lineage: 001-FR-020 -> 004-FR-002
133	
134	- **LOG-FR-EXT-LEVEL**: System MUST allow configuring an additional log level floor for external libraries (`log.external`) that applies to both file and TUI output.  
135	  Lineage: 004-FR-003
136	
137	- **LOG-FR-DEFAULTS**: System MUST apply sensible defaults when log levels are not specified in config (file: DEBUG, tui: INFO, external: WARNING).  
138	  Lineage: 004-FR-009
139	
140	- **LOG-FR-INVALID**: System MUST fail with a configuration error when invalid log level strings are provided in config.  
141	  Lineage: 004-FR-010
142	
143	#### External Library Logging
144	
145	- **LOG-FR-CAPTURE-EXT**: System MUST capture log messages from external libraries (asyncssh, etc.) and route them through the configured handlers.  
146	  Lineage: 004-FR-004
147	
148	#### Logging Infrastructure
149	
150	- **LOG-FR-STDLIB**: System MUST use Python's standard `logging` module as the foundation for all logging.  
151	  Lineage: 004-FR-005
152	
153	- **LOG-FR-EXCEPTION**: When a job raises an exception, the orchestrator MUST log the error at CRITICAL level, request termination of the currently-executing job (queued jobs never execute and do not receive termination requests), and halt sync immediately.  
154	  Lineage: 001-FR-019
155	
156	#### Output Format
157	
158	- **LOG-FR-FILE-PATH**: System MUST write all logs at configured file level or above to timestamped file in `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/sync-<timestamp>.log`.  
159	  Lineage: 001-FR-021
160	
161	- **LOG-FR-JSON**: System MUST preserve the current log file format: JSON Lines format (one JSON object per line with keys: timestamp in ISO8601 format, level, job, host, event, plus any additional context fields) for machine-readability. ~~hostname~~ removed from per-entry requirements; see LOG-FR-SESSION-HOSTNAMES.
162	  Lineage: 001-FR-022 → 004-FR-007 → #140
163	
164	- **LOG-FR-SESSION-HOSTNAMES**: System MUST log the source and target hostnames at session start, establishing the mapping between host roles ("source"/"target") and actual machine names.
165	  Lineage: #140
166	
167	- **LOG-FR-TUI-FORMAT**: System MUST preserve the current TUI format (colored, timestamped, structured) for human-readability. Format: `HH:MM:SS [LEVEL   ] [job] (host) message`.  
168	  Lineage: 001-FR-022 -> 004-FR-008
169	
170	- **LOG-FR-CONTEXT**: System MUST preserve structured context (key=value pairs) in log output.  
171	  Lineage: 004-FR-011
172	
173	#### Log Aggregation
174	
175	- **LOG-FR-AGGREGATE**: System MUST aggregate logs from both source-side orchestrator and target-side operations into unified log stream.  
176	  Lineage: 001-FR-023
177	
178	### Key Entities
179	
180	- **LogConfig**: Configuration entity holding three log level settings: `file`, `tui`, `external`.
181	  Lineage: 004-entities
182	
183	- **LogHandler**: Abstraction for output destinations (file, TUI) with their own level filtering.
184	  Lineage: 004-entities
185	
186	- **LogRecord**: Standard Python logging record with additional pc-switcher context (host, job, structured data).
187	  Lineage: 004-entities
188	
189	- **LogEntry**: Represents a logged event with timestamp, level, job name, message, and structured context data.
190	  Lineage: 001-entities
191	
192	## Success Criteria
193	
194	- **LOG-SC-CONFIG**: Users can configure all three log level settings (file, tui, external) through the config file without code changes.
195	  Lineage: 004-SC-001
196	
197	- **LOG-SC-EXT-APPEAR**: Log messages from asyncssh and other external libraries appear in log output when their level meets both the `external` threshold and the destination's (`file`/`tui`) threshold.
198	  Lineage: 004-SC-002
199	
200	- **LOG-SC-FILE-DEBUG**: Setting `log.file` to DEBUG and `log.tui` to INFO results in file containing debug messages that don't appear in TUI.
201	  Lineage: 004-SC-003
202	
203	- **LOG-SC-EXT-FILTER**: Setting `log.external` to WARNING filters out INFO/DEBUG messages from external libraries regardless of `file`/`tui` settings.
204	  Lineage: 004-SC-004
205	
206	- **LOG-SC-TUI-VISUAL**: TUI log output maintains identical visual format (colors, layout, timestamps) before and after migration.
207	  Lineage: 004-SC-005
208	
209	- **LOG-SC-JSON-STRUCT**: File log output maintains identical JSON structure before and after migration.
210	  Lineage: 004-SC-006
211	
212	- **LOG-SC-NO-REGRESS**: No regression in existing test suite after migration.
213	  Lineage: 004-SC-007
214	
215	- **LOG-SC-INVALID-FAIL**: Invalid log level in config causes startup failure with clear error message (consistent with other config errors).
216	  Lineage: 004-SC-008

- What happens when log volume is very high (e.g., FULL level during large sync)?
  - Performance should not degrade significantly; the logging pipeline should remain async.
  - Lineage: 004-edge-cases

## Requirements

### Functional Requirements

#### Log Level Hierarchy

- **LOG-FR-HIERARCHY**: System MUST implement six log levels with the following ordering and semantics: DEBUG > FULL > INFO > WARNING > ERROR > CRITICAL, where DEBUG is the most verbose. DEBUG includes all messages (FULL, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL, plus internal diagnostics). FULL includes all messages from INFO and below plus operational details, but excludes DEBUG-level internal diagnostics.  
  Lineage: 001-FR-018 -> 004-FR-006

**Log Level Definitions** (from most to least verbose):
- **DEBUG**: Most verbose level for internal diagnostics, including command outputs, detailed timings, internal state transitions, and all messages from lower levels (FULL, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL). Intended for deep troubleshooting and development.
- **FULL**: High-verbosity operational details including file-level operations (e.g., "Copying /home/user/document.txt", "Created snapshot pre-@home-20251115T143022") and all messages from lower levels (INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL). Excludes DEBUG-level internal diagnostics.
- **INFO**: High-level operation reporting for normal user visibility (e.g., "Starting job X", "Job X completed successfully", "Connection established") and all messages from lower levels (WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL).
- **WARNING**: Unexpected conditions that should be reviewed but don't indicate failure (e.g., config value using deprecated format, unusually large transfer size) and all messages from lower levels (ERROR, CRITICAL).
- **ERROR**: Recoverable errors that may impact sync quality but don't require abort (e.g., individual file copy failed, optional feature unavailable) and CRITICAL messages.
- **CRITICAL**: Unrecoverable errors requiring immediate sync abort (e.g., snapshot creation failed, target unreachable mid-sync, data corruption detected). Triggered when jobs raise an unhandled exception.

#### Level Configuration

- **LOG-FR-FILE-LEVEL**: System MUST allow configuring the log level floor for file output (`log.file`).  
  Lineage: 001-FR-020 -> 004-FR-001

- **LOG-FR-TUI-LEVEL**: System MUST allow configuring the log level floor for TUI output (`log.tui`).  
  Lineage: 001-FR-020 -> 004-FR-002

- **LOG-FR-EXT-LEVEL**: System MUST allow configuring an additional log level floor for external libraries (`log.external`) that applies to both file and TUI output.  
  Lineage: 004-FR-003

- **LOG-FR-DEFAULTS**: System MUST apply sensible defaults when log levels are not specified in config (file: DEBUG, tui: INFO, external: WARNING).  
  Lineage: 004-FR-009

- **LOG-FR-INVALID**: System MUST fail with a configuration error when invalid log level strings are provided in config.  
  Lineage: 004-FR-010

#### External Library Logging

- **LOG-FR-CAPTURE-EXT**: System MUST capture log messages from external libraries (asyncssh, etc.) and route them through the configured handlers.  
  Lineage: 004-FR-004

#### Logging Infrastructure

- **LOG-FR-STDLIB**: System MUST use Python's standard `logging` module as the foundation for all logging.  
  Lineage: 004-FR-005

- **LOG-FR-EXCEPTION**: When a job raises an exception, the orchestrator MUST log the error at CRITICAL level, request termination of the currently-executing job (queued jobs never execute and do not receive termination requests), and halt sync immediately.  
  Lineage: 001-FR-019

#### Output Format

- **LOG-FR-FILE-PATH**: System MUST write all logs at configured file level or above to timestamped file in `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/sync-<timestamp>.log`.  
  Lineage: 001-FR-021

- **LOG-FR-JSON**: System MUST preserve the current log file format: JSON Lines format (one JSON object per line with keys: timestamp in ISO8601 format, level, job, host, event, plus any additional context fields) for machine-readability. ~~hostname~~ removed from per-entry requirements; see LOG-FR-SESSION-HOSTNAMES.
  Lineage: 001-FR-022 → 004-FR-007 → #140

- **LOG-FR-SESSION-HOSTNAMES**: System MUST log the source and target hostnames at session start, establishing the mapping between host roles ("source"/"target") and actual machine names.
  Lineage: #140

- **LOG-FR-TUI-FORMAT**: System MUST preserve the current TUI format (colored, timestamped, structured) for human-readability. Format: `HH:MM:SS [LEVEL   ] [job] (host) message`.  
  Lineage: 001-FR-022 -> 004-FR-008

- **LOG-FR-CONTEXT**: System MUST preserve structured context (key=value pairs) in log output.  
  Lineage: 004-FR-011

#### Log Aggregation

- **LOG-FR-AGGREGATE**: System MUST aggregate logs from both source-side orchestrator and target-side operations into unified log stream.  
  Lineage: 001-FR-023

### Key Entities

- **LogConfig**: Configuration entity holding three log level settings: `file`, `tui`, `external`.
  Lineage: 004-entities

- **LogHandler**: Abstraction for output destinations (file, TUI) with their own level filtering.
  Lineage: 004-entities

- **LogRecord**: Standard Python logging record with additional pc-switcher context (host, job, structured data).
  Lineage: 004-entities

- **LogEntry**: Represents a logged event with timestamp, level, job name, message, and structured context data.
  Lineage: 001-entities

## Success Criteria

- **LOG-SC-CONFIG**: Users can configure all three log level settings (file, tui, external) through the config file without code changes.
  Lineage: 004-SC-001

- **LOG-SC-EXT-APPEAR**: Log messages from asyncssh and other external libraries appear in log output when their level meets both the `external` threshold and the destination's (`file`/`tui`) threshold.
  Lineage: 004-SC-002

- **LOG-SC-FILE-DEBUG**: Setting `log.file` to DEBUG and `log.tui` to INFO results in file containing debug messages that don't appear in TUI.
  Lineage: 004-SC-003

- **LOG-SC-EXT-FILTER**: Setting `log.external` to WARNING filters out INFO/DEBUG messages from external libraries regardless of `file`/`tui` settings.
  Lineage: 004-SC-004

- **LOG-SC-TUI-VISUAL**: TUI log output maintains identical visual format (colors, layout, timestamps) before and after migration.
  Lineage: 004-SC-005

- **LOG-SC-JSON-STRUCT**: File log output maintains identical JSON structure before and after migration.
  Lineage: 004-SC-006

- **LOG-SC-NO-REGRESS**: No regression in existing test suite after migration.
  Lineage: 004-SC-007

- **LOG-SC-INVALID-FAIL**: Invalid log level in config causes startup failure with clear error message (consistent with other config errors).
  Lineage: 004-SC-008

# Logging System

This document defines the current authoritative specification for pc-switcher's logging system.

## User Scenarios & Testing

### Comprehensive Logging System (LOG-US-SYSTEM)

Lineage: 001-US-4

The system implements a six-level logging hierarchy (DEBUG, FULL, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL) with independent level configuration for file logging, terminal UI display, and external library filtering. Log levels follow the ordering DEBUG > FULL > INFO > WARNING > ERROR > CRITICAL: DEBUG is the most verbose and includes all messages, while FULL is a high-verbosity operational level that does NOT include DEBUG-level diagnostics. Logs are written to timestamped files in `~/.local/share/pc-switcher/logs/` on the source machine. All operations (core orchestrator, individual jobs, target-side scripts) contribute to the unified log stream.

**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by:
1. Running sync with various log level configurations
2. Verifying file contains events at configured level and above
3. Confirming terminal shows only events at CLI log level and above
4. Checking log format includes timestamp, level, job name, and message
5. Validating that both source and target operations contribute to unified log

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** user configures `log.file: FULL` and `log.tui: INFO`, **When** sync runs and a job logs at DEBUG level, **Then** the message does NOT appear in the log file nor in the terminal UI (DEBUG is excluded by FULL)

2. **Given** user configures `log.file: INFO`, **When** sync runs and a job logs at FULL level (e.g., "Copying /home/user/file.txt"), **Then** the message does not appear in either log file or terminal UI

3. *(Removed)*

The hostname mapping (source hostname and target hostname) is logged once at session start, not in every entry.

# Python Development Conventions

Apply to Python code/projects. New or owner-controlled projects: use defaults. Existing: follow project config (Python version, deps, tests, lint, CI) unless explicitly migrating. Use modern standards for supported version.

## Python Version and Modern Practices

- New Python projects: target >=3.14
- Existing projects: use modern syntax for configured version
- Use modern type syntax: e.g. `str | None` instead of `Optional[str]`, `list[str]` instead of `List[str]`
- Include `from __future__ import annotations` when improves annotation behavior for Python version
- Use `@override` for method overrides when available in Python version
- Prefer `StrEnum` for string-based enums when available in Python version
- Prefer `Path` from pathlib for local filesystem paths

## Project Setup and Developer Workflows

- MUST use uv for running code, managing dependencies in new projects
- Existing projects: use project's workflow. Don't replace Poetry/Hatch/tox/Makefiles unless asked
- Use plain uv commands: `uv sync`, `uv run ...`, `uv add`, etc. Avoid `uv pip`. NEVER use `uv pip install` unless project intentionally uses uv's pip-compatible interface
- Use `codespell` for checking typos in code/comments
- Use `ruff` for linting, formatting
- Use `basedpyright` for type checking
- Use `pytest` for testing

## Code Quality Standards

- Full type annotations for function signatures, modern syntax per Python version
- Import types from correct modules (e.g., use `collections.abc` not `typing` for collection types)
- Proper error handling with specific exception types, meaningful messages
- Single responsibility principle, keep functions focused
- Use context managers for resource management
- Prefer composition over inheritance
- Self-documenting code with meaningful names
- Follow PEP 8 (max 119 chars), use Python strengths, avoid anti-patterns

## Documentation Philosophy

- Document architectural decisions, design patterns
- Concise docstrings: focus on "why" not obvious "what"
- Explain complex logic rationales inline when needed
- Use backticks for variable/parameter names in docstrings
- Avoid repetitive or obvious comments

## Testing Strategy

- Small focused tests, clear locality. Use repo's layout (colocated, `tests/` tree, doctests)
- Avoid trivial tests that test language itself
- Simple assertions over complex pytest fixtures when possible
- Test edge cases, errors, boundary values
- Mock external dependencies, I/O operations appropriately
- Mocked integration behavior: add real-dep integration coverage where practical
- Never use `assert False` - raise `AssertionError` with explanation instead

## Performance Optimization Techniques

- Use appropriate data structures (sets for membership, deque for queues, etc.)
- Implement generators for memory-efficient iteration over large datasets
- Apply caching strategies where beneficial
- Utilize built-in functions/libraries (itertools, collections, functools)
- Consider algorithmic complexity, choose efficient algorithms
- Profile code using cProfile, line_profiler, or memory_profiler before optimizing

## Error Handling and Type Checking

- Use `# pyright: ignore` sparingly, always with explanation
- Raise `AssertionError` with descriptive messages for invariant violations
- Handle exceptions at appropriate levels, avoid bare except
- Use specific exception types that convey meaning

## File Operations Best Practices

- Prefer `Path` objects for local filesystem paths
- Atomic file writing for user data, config, state or other where partial output harmful
- Use context managers for file operations
- Handle encoding explicitly (default UTF-8)

## Core Principles

- Pragmatic: avoid over-engineering, premature optimization
- Leverage stdlib first. Use third-party packages sparingly
- Code clarity over clever tricks
- Aim for functional, clean, maintainable, performant code

- Utilize built-in functions/libraries (itertools, collections, functools)
- Consider algorithmic complexity, choose efficient algorithms
- Profile code using cProfile, line_profiler, or memory_profiler before optimizing

## Error Handling and Type Checking

- Use `# pyright: ignore` sparingly, always with explanation
- Raise `AssertionError` with descriptive messages for invariant violations
- Handle exceptions at appropriate levels, avoid bare except
- Use specific exception types that convey meaning

## File Operations Best Practices

- Prefer `Path` objects for local filesystem paths
- Atomic file writing for user data, config, state or other where partial output harmful
- Use context managers for file operations
- Handle encoding explicitly (default UTF-8)

## Core Principles

- Pragmatic: avoid over-engineering, premature optimization
- Leverage stdlib first. Use third-party packages sparingly
- Code clarity over clever tricks
- Aim for functional, clean, maintainable, performant code

# Python Development Commands

New or owner-controlled projects → uv. Existing repos → follow project's setup unless asked.

## Package Management with uv

Prefer uv (new projects). Skip `uv pip` unless project uses it. Don't replace other package managers without ask.

```bash
# Initialize new project
uv init

# Add dependencies
uv add package_name
uv add --dev ipykernel pytest ruff basedpyright  # dev dependencies

# Sync dependencies
uv sync

# Show installed packages
uv tree
uv tree --package package_name

# Run commands in virtual environment
uv run python script.py
uv run pytest
```

## Testing with pytest

```bash
# Run all tests
uv run pytest

# Run tests with coverage
uv run pytest --cov=src --cov-report=term-missing

# Run specific test file or function
uv run pytest tests/test_module.py
uv run pytest tests/test_module.py::test_function

# Run tests matching pattern
uv run pytest -k "pattern"

# Run tests with verbose output
uv run pytest -v

# Run tests and stop on first failure
uv run pytest -x
```

## Linting and Formatting with ruff

```bash
# Format code
uv run ruff format .

# Check linting issues
uv run ruff check .

# Fix auto-fixable issues
uv run ruff check --fix .

# Check specific file
uv run ruff check path/to/file.py
```

## Type Checking with basedpyright

```bash
# Type check entire project
uv run basedpyright

# Type check specific file
uv run basedpyright path/to/file.py
```

## Code Quality Checks

```bash
# Run all quality checks (typical CI pipeline)
uv run ruff format --check .
uv run ruff check .
uv run basedpyright
uv run pytest

# Check for common typos
uv run codespell
```

## Profiling and Performance

```bash
# Profile with cProfile
uv run python -m cProfile -s cumtime script.py

# Profile with line_profiler
uv run kernprof -l -v script.py

# Memory profiling
uv run python -m memory_profiler script.py
```

## Common Development Patterns

```bash
# Check outdated packages
uv tree --outdated

# Clean pyc files and caches only when explicitly requested
find . -type d -name "__pycache__" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type d -name ".pytest_cache" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type d -name ".ruff_cache" -exec rm -rf {} +
find . -type f -name "*.pyc" -delete
```

input.content:
# Reading Sync Logs

At `log.file: FULL`, folder-sync jobs log one line per changed file. Each line comes from rsync's
`--itemize-changes` and is prefixed with the folder path:

```
/home/user: <f+++++++++ Documents/notes.txt
/home/user: cd+++++++++ Documents/
/home/user: >f.st...... Pictures/photo.jpg
```

The 11-character code before the filename is rsync's itemized-change flag field, of the form
`YXcstpoguax`.

## Position 1 — update type

What is happening to the item:

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `<`  | file transferred **to** the remote (sending) |
| `>`  | file transferred **from** the remote (receiving) |
| `c`  | local **change/creation** — directory, symlink, or device created |
| `h`  | hard link |
| `.`  | no transfer; only attributes changed |
| `*`  | a message follows instead of a code (e.g. `*deleting`) |

## Position 2 — file type

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `f`  | regular file |
| `d`  | directory |
| `L`  | symlink |
| `D`  | device |
| `S`  | special file (socket, fifo) |

## Positions 3–11 — which attributes differ

Each position is the attribute's letter (it changed), `.` (unchanged), or `+` (item is being
created, so nothing to compare):

| Pos | Char | Attribute |
|-----|------|-----------|
| 3   | `c`  | checksum/content differs |
| 4   | `s`  | size differs |
| 5   | `t`  | modification time differs (`T` = time set to transfer-time) |
| 6   | `p`  | permissions differ |
| 7   | `o`  | owner differs |
| 8   | `g`  | group differs |
| 9   | `u`  | access/modify/create time (reporting) |
| 10  | `a`  | ACL differs |
| 11  | `x`  | extended attributes differ |

When an item is newly created, every attribute slot shows `+` (there is nothing on the target to
compare against).

## Worked examples

- `cd+++++++++ Documents/` — a **directory** that did not exist on the target and was just
  **created** (`c` = created locally, `d` = directory, all `+` = new).
- `<f+++++++++ notes.txt` — a brand-new **file transferred to the target** (`<` = sent to remote,
  `f` = regular file, all `+` = new).
- `>f.st...... photo.jpg` — an **existing file updated** because its **size** (`s`) and
  **modification time** (`t`) differ.
- `.d..t...... Pictures/` — a directory left in place, only its **timestamp** was adjusted.
- `*deleting old.txt` — the file was **deleted** on the target.

## Reference

Full specification: the [rsync manual page](https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1),
under `--itemize-changes` (`-i`).

/home/user: cd+++++++++ Documents/
/home/user: >f.st...... Pictures/photo.jpg
```

The 11-character code before the filename is rsync's itemized-change flag field, of the form `YXcstpoguax`.

## Position 1 — update type

What is happening to the item:

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `<`  | file transferred **to** the remote (sending) |
| `>`  | file transferred **from** the remote (receiving) |
| `c`  | local **change/creation** — directory, symlink, or device created |
| `h`  | hard link |
| `.`  | no transfer; only attributes changed |
| `*`  | a message follows instead of a code (e.g. `*deleting`) |

## Position 2 — file type

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `f`  | regular file |
| `d`  | directory |
| `L`  | symlink |
| `D`  | device |
| `S`  | special file (socket, fifo) |

## Positions 3–11 — which attributes differ

Each position is the attribute's letter (it changed), `.` (unchanged), or `+` (item is being created, so nothing to compare):

| Pos | Char | Attribute |
|-----|------|-----------|
| 3   | `c`  | checksum/content differs |
| 4   | `s`  | size differs |
| 5   | `t`  | modification time differs (`T` = time set to transfer-time) |
| 6   | `p`  | permissions differ |
| 7   | `o`  | owner differs |
| 8   | `g`  | group differs |
| 9   | `u`  | access/modify/create time (reporting) |
| 10  | `a`  | ACL differs |
| 11  | `x`  | extended attributes differ |

When an item is newly created, every attribute slot shows `+` (there is nothing on the target to compare against).

## Worked examples

- `cd+++++++++ Documents/` — a **directory** that did not exist on the target and was just **created** (`c` = created locally, `d` = directory, all `+` = new).
- `<f+++++++++ notes.txt` — a brand-new **file transferred to the target** (`<` = sent to remote, `f` = regular file, all `+` = new).
- `>f.st...... photo.jpg` — an **existing file updated** because its **size** (`s`) and **modification time** (`t`) differ.
- `.d..t...... Pictures/` — a directory left in place, only its **timestamp** was adjusted.
- `*deleting old.txt` — the file was **deleted** on the target.

## Reference

Full specification: the [rsync manual page](https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1), under `--itemize-changes` (`-i`).

# Reading Sync Logs

At `log.file: FULL`, folder-sync jobs log one line per changed file. Each line comes from rsync's
`--itemize-changes` and is prefixed with the folder path:

```
/home/user: <f+++++++++ Documents/notes.txt
/home/user: cd+++++++++ Documents/
/home/user: >f.st...... Pictures/photo.jpg
```

The 11-character code before the filename is rsync's itemized-change flag field, of the form
`YXcstpoguax`.

## Position 1 — update type

What is happening to the item:

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `<`  | file transferred **to** the remote (sending) |
| `>`  | file transferred **from** the remote (receiving) |
| `c`  | local **change/creation** — directory, symlink, or device created |
| `h`  | hard link |
| `.`  | no transfer; only attributes changed |
| `*`  | a message follows instead of a code (e.g. `*deleting`) |

## Position 2 — file type

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `f`  | regular file |
| `d`  | directory |
| `L`  | symlink |
| `D`  | device |
| `S`  | special file (socket, fifo) |

## Positions 3–11 — which attributes differ

Each position is the attribute's letter (it changed), `.` (unchanged), or `+` (item is being
created, so nothing to compare):

| Pos | Char | Attribute |
|-----|------|-----------|
| 3   | `c`  | checksum/content differs |
| 4   | `s`  | size differs |
| 5   | `t`  | modification time differs (`T` = time set to transfer-time) |
| 6   | `p`  | permissions differ |
| 7   | `o`  | owner differs |
| 8   | `g`  | group differs |
| 9   | `u`  | access/modify/create time (reporting) |
| 10  | `a`  | ACL differs |
| 11  | `x`  | extended attributes differ |

When an item is newly created, every attribute slot shows `+` (there is nothing on the target to
compare against).

- `<f+++++++++ notes.txt` — a brand-new **file transferred to the target** (`<` = sent to remote,

Documented. Summary of what I added:

- **`docs/ops/reading-sync-logs.md`** — the reference. Explains the 11-char `YXcstpoguax` itemize field (all three position groups), the `+`-means-newly-created rule, and worked examples for `cd+++++++++`, `<f+++++++++`, plus a few common existing-file/delete cases. Ends with a link to the official [rsync man page](https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1) under `--itemize-changes`.
- **`docs/README.md`** — indexed it in the ops section.
- **`src/pcswitcher/jobs/folder_sync.py:500`** — added a one-line pointer to the new doc next to the existing inline change-type comment.

One thing worth flagging: position 9 (`u`) I documented as "access/modify/create time" per the rsync spec, but rsync's exact letter there depends on which time-reporting flags are in effect (`u`/`n`/`b`). If your `folder_sync` command doesn't pass `--atimes`/`--crtimes`, that slot will effectively never show anything but `.`, so it's harmless — I kept the general spec meaning rather than tailoring it to the current flag set, since the doc is a lookup reference for whatever rsync emits.

356	    def _build_rsync_cmd(self, folder: FolderEntry, dry_run: bool, delete: bool = True) -> str:
357	        """Build the rsync shell command for syncing a single folder.
358	
359	        Produces a command that:
360	        - Runs as root on source via `sudo -E rsync` (preserves SSH_AUTH_SOCK
361	          so the ssh agent socket remains accessible).
362	        - Elevates to root on target via `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` over the
363	          normal-user SSH connection (D-05); root SSH login stays disabled.
364	        - Uses the D-13 flag baseline: -aAXHS + --numeric-ids + --delete.
365	        - Adds --dry-run only when `dry_run` is True (D-12).
366	        - Omits --delete when `delete` is False: `execute` runs a no-delete seeding
367	          pass first (when the tree has per-directory filters) so every source
368	          `.pcswitcher-filter` reaches the target before the deleting mirror — a
369	          dir-merge rule only protects the target once the filter file is on the
370	          receiver, else --delete would remove (not protect) the files it names.
371	        - Never includes --delete-excluded (excluded files must survive on target
372	          — D-06) or --checksum (rsync's built-in verification is trusted — D-14).
373	
374	        All config-derived values (folder path, exclude patterns, target hostname)
375	        are shlex.quote'd to prevent shell injection (T-05-01).
376	
377	        The SSH transport and target-side sudo elevation are built by
378	        `_transport_args`.  The filter chain (runtime excludes → central merge →
379	        dir-merge) is identical with and without --delete, so both passes respect the
380	        central `filter_file` and per-directory `.pcswitcher-filter` files exactly.
381	        """
382	        parts = [
383	            "sudo",
384	            "-E",
385	            # Force the C locale so rsync's --info=progress2 byte counter is
386	            # printed ungrouped.  Under a grouping locale (e.g. LC_NUMERIC=nl_BE)
387	            # rsync prints "80.153.795.479"; the progress2 regex captures the
388	            # whole token and float() then rejects the thousands separators,
389	            # aborting the sync over a purely cosmetic figure (WR-01).  `env`
390	            # runs after sudo so it sets the locale for rsync as root regardless
391	            # of sudo's own env policy.
392	            "env",
393	            "LC_ALL=C",
394	            "rsync",
395	            "-aAXHS",
396	            "--numeric-ids",
397	            "--info=progress2",
398	            f"--out-format={shlex.quote('%i %n%L')}",
399	            "--partial",
400	            "--mkpath",
401	        ]
402	
403	        if delete:
404	            parts.append("--delete")
405	
406	        if dry_run:
407	            parts.append("--dry-run")
408	
409	        # SSH transport + target-side sudo elevation (shared with the pre-seed pass).
410	        parts.extend(self._transport_args())
411	
412	        # GLOBAL-FIRST filter precedence: runtime excludes (un-overridable, ADR-016)
413	        # come first; the folder's central `merge` filter (when configured) comes
414	        # next so its rules win over any per-directory file under first-match-wins;
415	        # the tree-wide `dir-merge /.pcswitcher-filter` is always last and gives
416	        # users a per-directory (gitignore-like) authoring surface. DO NOT add
417	        # --delete-excluded — excluded files (e.g. .ssh/id_*, .config/tailscale)
418	        # must survive on the target (D-06).
419	        parts.extend(self._runtime_exclude_filters(folder.path))
420	
421	        expanded_filter_file = folder.expanded_filter_file()
422	        if expanded_filter_file is not None:
423	            parts.append(f"--filter={shlex.quote(f'merge {expanded_filter_file}')}")
424	        parts.append(f"--filter={shlex.quote('dir-merge /.pcswitcher-filter')}")
425	
426	        # Source: trailing slash syncs contents, not the directory itself.
427	        src = shlex.quote(folder.path.rstrip("/") + "/")
428	        parts.append(src)
429	
430	        # Destination: <target_hostname>:<path>/ — rsync opens its own SSH
431	        # connection using ~/.ssh/config (ADR-002); the colon is rsync's own
432	        # remote-host separator, not a shell metacharacter.
433	        dst_raw = f"{self.context.target_hostname}:{folder.path.rstrip('/') + '/'}"
434	        parts.append(shlex.quote(dst_raw))
435	
436	        return " ".join(parts)
437	
438	    async def _stream_rsync(
439	        self,
440	        chunks: AsyncIterator[bytes],

def _build_rsync_cmd(self, folder: FolderEntry, dry_run: bool, delete: bool = True) -> str:
        """Build the rsync shell command for syncing a single folder.

        Produces a command that:
        - Runs as root on source via `sudo -E rsync` (preserves SSH_AUTH_SOCK
          so the ssh agent socket remains accessible).
        - Elevates to root on target via `--rsync-path='sudo rsync'` over the
          normal-user SSH connection (D-05); root SSH login stays disabled.
        - Uses the D-13 flag baseline: -aAXHS + --numeric-ids + --delete.
        - Adds --dry-run only when `dry_run` is True (D-12).
        - Omits --delete when `delete` is False: `execute` runs a no-delete seeding
          pass first (when the tree has per-directory filters) so every source
          `.pcswitcher-filter` reaches the target before the deleting mirror — a
          dir-merge rule only protects the target once the filter file is on the
          receiver, else --delete would remove (not protect) the files it names.
        - Never includes --delete-excluded (excluded files must survive on target
          — D-06) or --checksum (rsync's built-in verification is trusted — D-14).

        All config-derived values (folder path, exclude patterns, target hostname)
        are shlex.quote'd to prevent shell injection (T-05-01).

        The SSH transport and target-side sudo elevation are built by
        `_transport_args`.  The filter chain (runtime excludes → central merge →
        dir-merge) is identical with and without --delete, so both passes respect the
        central `filter_file` and per-directory `.pcswitcher-filter` files exactly.
        """
        parts = [
            "sudo",
            "-E",
            # Force the C locale so rsync's --info=progress2 byte counter is
            # printed ungrouped.  Under a grouping locale (e.g. LC_NUMERIC=nl_BE)
            # rsync prints "80.153.795.479"; the progress2 regex captures the
            # whole token and float() then rejects the thousands separators,
            # aborting the sync over a purely cosmetic figure (WR-01).  `env`
            # runs after sudo so it sets the locale for rsync as root regardless
            # of sudo's own env policy.
            "env",
            "LC_ALL=C",
            "rsync",
            "-aAXHS",
            "--numeric-ids",
            "--info=progress2",
            f"--out-format={shlex.quote('%i %n%L')}",
            "--partial",
            "--mkpath",
        ]

        if delete:
            parts.append("--delete")

        if dry_run:
            parts.append("--dry-run")

        # SSH transport + target-side sudo elevation (shared with the pre-seed pass).
        parts.extend(self._transport_args())

        # GLOBAL-FIRST filter precedence: runtime excludes (un-overridable, ADR-016)
        # come first; the folder's central `merge` filter (when configured) comes
        # next so its rules win over any per-directory file under first-match-wins;
        # the tree-wide `dir-merge /.pcswitcher-filter` is always last and gives
        # users a per-directory (gitignore-like) authoring surface. DO NOT add
        # --delete-excluded — excluded files (e.g. .ssh/id_*, .config/tailscale)
        # must survive on the target (D-06).
        parts.extend(self._runtime_exclude_filters(folder.path))

        expanded_filter_file = folder.expanded_filter_file()
        if expanded_filter_file is not None:
            parts.append(f"--filter={shlex.quote(f'merge {expanded_filter_file}')}")
        parts.append(f"--filter={shlex.quote('dir-merge /.pcswitcher-filter')}")

        # Source: trailing slash syncs contents, not the directory itself.
        src = shlex.quote(folder.path.rstrip("/") + "/")
        parts.append(src)

        # Destination: <target_hostname>:<path>/ — rsync opens its own SSH
        # connection using ~/.ssh/config (ADR-002); the colon is rsync's own
        # remote-host separator, not a shell metacharacter.
        dst_raw = f"{self.context.target_hostname}:{folder.path.rstrip('/') + '/'}"
        parts.append(shlex.quote(dst_raw))

        return " ".join(parts)

    async def _stream_rsync(
        self,
        chunks: AsyncIterator[bytes],
        folder: FolderEntry,
    ) -> tuple[int, int, int]:
        """Stream rsync stdout, updating TUI progress and logging per-file events.

        Handles `--info=progress2` output (carriage-return-delimited) and
        `--out-format` per-file lines (newline-delimited) in the same byte
        stream (RESEARCH Pitfall 2 — do not use readline-based iteration here).

        Decoupled from the subprocess: consumes any `AsyncIterator[bytes]` so
        it can be unit-tested with a fake async generator.

        Args:
            chunks: Async byte-chunk source (e.g. `proc.read_stdout_chunks()`).
            folder: The folder being synced (used for log context).

        Returns:
            Tuple of (files_transferred, bytes_transferred, files_deleted).
            bytes_transferred is a best-effort count based on the last progress
            line (progress2 doesn't expose a precise cumulative byte count).
        """
        files_xfr = 0
        bytes_xfr = 0
        files_deleted = 0
        buf = b""

        async for chunk in chunks:
            buf += chunk
            # Split on both \r and \n to handle progress2 (uses \r) and
            # per-file --out-format lines (use \n) in the same stream.
            parts = re.split(rb"[\r\n]", buf)
            buf = parts[-1]  # Keep incomplete trailing fragment for next chunk.
            for fragment in parts[:-1]:
                line = fragment.decode(errors="replace").strip()
                if not line:
                    continue
                m = _PROGRESS2_RE.search(line)
                if m:
                    # Progress2 line: update TUI (D-15) and capture transferred bytes (WR-01).
                    # Group numbering after adding the size capture group:
                    #   1=size  2=percent  3=files_xfr  4=total_check
                    # Last progress line wins — rsync emits these as running totals so the
                    # final line is the best approximation of the cumulative byte count.
                    bytes_xfr = self._parse_size_to_bytes(m.group(1))
                    pct = int(m.group(2))
                    files_xfr = int(m.group(3))

# Reading Sync Logs

At `log.file: FULL`, folder-sync jobs log one line per changed item. Each line is a prefixed copy of rsync's per-file output. pc-switcher runs rsync with `--out-format='%i %n%L'`, so the line is:

```
<itemize-code> <path><symlink-or-hardlink-suffix>
```

and pc-switcher prepends the folder path. Real examples:

```
/home/user: <f+++++++++ Documents/notes.txt
/home/user: cd+++++++++ Documents/
/home/user: <f.st...... Pictures/photo.jpg
/home/user: cL+++++++++ .bashrc -> /etc/skel/.bashrc
/home/user: *deleting   Downloads/old.iso
```

The leading token is rsync's 11-character itemized-change field, of the form `YXcstpoguax`. `%L` appends ` -> target` for a symlink or ` => target` for a hard link; it is empty otherwise.

## Which codes this tool can actually emit

The exact rsync flag baseline is fixed in `_build_rsync_cmd` (`src/pcswitcher/jobs/folder_sync.py`):

```
rsync -aAXHS --numeric-ids --out-format='%i %n%L' [--delete]
```

`-a` expands to `-rlptgoD`, so permissions, mod-times, owner, group, devices, and specials are all preserved; `-A` ACLs, `-X` xattrs, `-H` hard links, `-S` sparse. There is **no** `--checksum` (D-14), **no** `--atimes`/`--crtimes`, and the transfer is always source→target. That pins down every position below.

### Position 1 — update type

| Char | Appears? | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|
| `<`  | yes | item sent to the target — the normal transfer marker |
| `c`  | yes | created locally on the target: a directory, symlink, device, or special file |
| `h`  | yes | item is a hard link to another item (`-H`) |
| `.`  | yes | no transfer; only attributes changed |
| `*`  | yes | a message follows instead of a code — in practice `*deleting` (`--delete` pass) |
| `>`  | **no** | would mean "received from remote"; this tool only sends |

### Position 2 — file type

All of these can appear (archive mode transfers every type):

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `f`  | regular file |
| `d`  | directory |
| `L`  | symlink |
| `D`  | device |
| `S`  | special file (socket, fifo) |

### Positions 3–11 — which attribute differs

Each slot shows the attribute's letter (it changed), `.` (unchanged), a space (identical item, shown alongside other changes), or `+` (item is newly created, so every slot is `+`).

| Pos | Char | Attribute | Appears with this tool's flags? |
|-----|------|-----------|----------------------------------|
| 3   | `c`  | checksum/content differs | Only for symlink/device/special value changes. **Never for regular files** — that needs `--checksum`, which this tool does not pass. |
| 4   | `s`  | size differs | yes |
| 5   | `t`  | mod-time differs | yes — always `t`, never `T` (`-t` is on, so times are preserved, not set-to-transfer-time) |
| 6   | `p`  | permissions differ | yes (`-p`) |
| 7   | `o`  | owner (uid) differs | yes (`-o`); uid is numeric (`--numeric-ids`) |
| 8   | `g`  | group (gid) differs | yes (`-g`); gid is numeric (`--numeric-ids`) |
| 9   | `u`/`n`/`b` | access/create time | **Never** — requires `--atimes`/`--crtimes`, not passed. Always `.` |
| 10  | `a`  | ACL differs | yes (`-A`) |
| 11  | `x`  | extended attributes differ | yes (`-X`) |

So a regular-file change in these logs is driven by positions 4–8, 10, 11 (size, time, perms, owner, group, ACL, xattr); position 3 and position 9 stay `.` for regular files.

## Worked examples

- `cd+++++++++ Documents/` — a **directory** that did not exist on the target and was just **created** (`c` = created locally, `d` = directory, all `+` = new).
- `<f+++++++++ notes.txt` — a brand-new **file sent to the target** (`<` = sent, `f` = regular file, all `+` = new).
- `<f.st...... photo.jpg` — an **existing file re-sent** because its **size** (`s`) and **mod-time** (`t`) differ; position 3 stays `.` because no checksum is computed.
- `.d..t...... Pictures/` — directory left in place, only its **timestamp** was adjusted (no transfer).
- `.f...p..... script.sh` — file content identical, only **permissions** (`p`) changed.
- `cL+++++++++ .bashrc -> /etc/skel/.bashrc` — a new **symlink** created, pointing at the shown target (`%L` suffix).
- `*deleting old.iso` — the item was **deleted** on the target (only on the `--delete` mirror pass).

## Reference

Full specification: the [rsync manual page](https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1), under `--itemize-changes` (`-i`) and `--out-format`.

input.content:
# Reading Sync Logs

When you set `log.file: FULL`, pc-switcher records one line per changed item during a folder sync. Each line looks like this:

```
/home/user: <f+++++++++ Documents/notes.txt
/home/user: cd+++++++++ Documents/
/home/user: <f.st...... Pictures/photo.jpg
/home/user: cL+++++++++ .bashrc -> /etc/skel/.bashrc
/home/user: *deleting   Downloads/old.iso
```

Reading left to right: the synced folder, then an 11-character change code, then the item's path (with ` -> target` shown for symlinks). The change code tells you what happened to that item and why it was copied.

## The change code

The code has the form `YXcstpoguax`. The first two characters describe the item; the remaining nine describe which of its attributes differed from the target.

### Character 1 — what happened

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `<`  | the item was **copied to the target** |
| `c`  | a directory, symlink, device, or special file was **created** on the target |
| `h`  | the item is a **hard link** to another item |
| `.`  | nothing was copied — only attributes changed |
| `*`  | a message follows instead of a code (you'll see `*deleting` when an item is removed from the target) |

pc-switcher only ever copies **from this machine to the target**, so you will never see `>` (which would mean the reverse).

### Character 2 — the kind of item

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `f`  | regular file |
| `d`  | directory |
| `L`  | symlink |
| `D`  | device |
| `S`  | special file (socket, pipe) |

### Characters 3–11 — what differed

For a **newly created** item there is nothing on the target to compare against, so every slot shows `+` (that's why new files read `<f+++++++++`). Otherwise each slot shows its letter if that attribute differed, or `.` if it matched:

| Char | Attribute |
|------|-----------|
| `s`  | size |
| `t`  | modification time |
| `p`  | permissions |
| `o`  | owner |
| `g`  | group |
| `a`  | access-control list (ACL) |
| `x`  | extended attributes |

(The two remaining slots — position 3 and position 9 — stay `.` for ordinary files in pc-switcher's sync.)

## Worked examples

- `cd+++++++++ Documents/` — a **new directory** that didn't exist on the target.
- `<f+++++++++ notes.txt` — a **new file** copied to the target.
- `<f.st...... photo.jpg` — an **existing file re-copied** because its **size** (`s`) and **modification time** (`t`) differed.
- `.f...p..... script.sh` — the file's contents matched; only its **permissions** (`p`) changed, so nothing was transferred.
- `.d..t...... Pictures/` — an existing directory whose **timestamp** was adjusted.
- `cL+++++++++ .bashrc -> /etc/skel/.bashrc` — a **new symlink** pointing at the shown target.
- `*deleting old.iso` — the item was **removed from the target** because it no longer exists here.

## More detail

These codes come from `rsync`. Their full specification is in the [rsync manual page](https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1), under `--itemize-changes`.

# Reading Sync Logs

At `log.file: FULL`, folder-sync jobs log one line per changed item. Each line is a prefixed copy of rsync's per-file output. pc-switcher runs rsync with `--out-format='%i %n%L'`, so the line is:

```
<itemize-code> <path><symlink-or-hardlink-suffix>
```

and pc-switcher prepends the folder path. Real examples:

```
/home/user: <f+++++++++ Documents/notes.txt
/home/user: cd+++++++++ Documents/
/home/user: <f.st...... Pictures/photo.jpg
/home/user: cL+++++++++ .bashrc -> /etc/skel/.bashrc
/home/user: *deleting   Downloads/old.iso
```

The leading token is rsync's 11-character itemized-change field, of the form `YXcstpoguax`. `%L` appends ` -> target` for a symlink or ` => target` for a hard link; it is empty otherwise.

## Which codes this tool can actually emit

The exact rsync flag baseline is fixed in `_build_rsync_cmd` (`src/pcswitcher/jobs/folder_sync.py`):

```
rsync -aAXHS --numeric-ids --out-format='%i %n%L' [--delete]
```

`-a` expands to `-rlptgoD`, so permissions, mod-times, owner, group, devices, and specials are all preserved; `-A` ACLs, `-X` xattrs, `-H` hard links, `-S` sparse. There is **no** `--checksum` (D-14), **no** `--atimes`/`--crtimes`, and the transfer is always source→target. That pins down every position below.

### Position 1 — update type

| Char | Appears? | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|
| `<`  | yes | item sent to the target — the normal transfer marker |
| `c`  | yes | created locally on the target: a directory, symlink, device, or special file |
| `h`  | yes | item is a hard link to another item (`-H`) |
| `.`  | yes | no transfer; only attributes changed |
| `*`  | yes | a message follows instead of a code — in practice `*deleting` (`--delete` pass) |
| `>`  | **no** | would mean "received from remote"; this tool only sends |

### Position 2 — file type

All of these can appear (archive mode transfers every type):

| Char | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `f`  | regular file |
| `d`  | directory |
| `L`  | symlink |
| `D`  | device |
| `S`  | special file (socket, fifo) |

### Positions 3–11 — which attribute differs

Each slot shows the attribute's letter (it changed), `.` (unchanged), a space (identical item, shown alongside other changes), or `+` (item is newly created, so every slot is `+`).

| Pos | Char | Attribute | Appears with this tool's flags? |
|-----|------|-----------|----------------------------------|
| 3   | `c`  | checksum/content differs | Only for symlink/device/special value changes. **Never for regular files** — that needs `--checksum`, which this tool does not pass. |
| 4   | `s`  | size differs | yes |
| 5   | `t`  | mod-time differs | yes — always `t`, never `T` (`-t` is on, so times are preserved, not set-to-transfer-time) |
| 6   | `p`  | permissions differ | yes (`-p`) |
| 7   | `o`  | owner (uid) differs | yes (`-o`); uid is numeric (`--numeric-ids`) |
| 8   | `g`  | group (gid) differs | yes (`-g`); gid is numeric (`--numeric-ids`) |
| 9   | `u`/`n`/`b` | access/create time | **Never** — requires `--atimes`/`--crtimes`, not passed. Always `.` |
| 10  | `a`  | ACL differs | yes (`-A`) |
| 11  | `x`  | extended attributes differ | yes (`-X`) |

So a regular-file change in these logs is driven by positions 4–8, 10, 11 (size, time, perms, owner, group, ACL, xattr); position 3 and position 9 stay `.` for regular files.

## Worked examples

- `cd+++++++++ Documents/` — a **directory** that did not exist on the target and was just **created** (`c` = created locally, `d` = directory, all `+` = new).
- `<f+++++++++ notes.txt` — a brand-new **file sent to the target** (`<` = sent, `f` = regular file, all `+` = new).
- `<f.st...... photo.jpg` — an **existing file re-sent** because its **size** (`s`) and **mod-time** (`t`) differ; position 3 stays `.` because no checksum is computed.
- `.d..t...... Pictures/` — directory left in place, only its **timestamp** was adjusted (no transfer).
- `.f...p..... script.sh` — file content identical, only **permissions** (`p`) changed.
- `cL+++++++++ .bashrc -> /etc/skel/.bashrc` — a new **symlink** created, pointing at the shown target (`%L` suffix).
- `*deleting old.iso` — the item was **deleted** on the target (only on the `--delete` mirror pass).

## Reference

Full specification: the [rsync manual page](https://download.samba.org/pub/rsync/rsync.1), under `--itemize-changes` (`-i`) and `--out-format`.
